page.title: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) — RepurpOS | OpenSourceMedicine
page.description: Structured alterations and therapeutics for Irritable Bowel Syndrome: molecular targets, clinical biomarkers, phenotypes, and ranked drugs from Open Targets, HPO, ChEMBL, and DGIdb.
page.hero: Disease intelligence for Irritable Bowel Syndrome: 111 alterations and 228 ranked therapeutics from curated public databases.

gmi_articles.title: A review of meditation and irritable bowel syndrome.
Probiotics are effective and safe for IBS patients.
Aloe vera is effective and safe for the treatment of patients with irritable bowel syndrome.
Current evidence shows particular combinations, species or strains of probiotics are effective for overall IBS symptoms.
Peppermint oil was shown to be a safe and effective therapy for pain and global symptoms in adults with irritable bowel syndrome.
Probiotics have beneficial effects on IBS, which can improve the patients' symptoms and with less adverse reaction.

A brief psycho-educational group intervention is efficacious in changing cognitions and fears about the symptoms of the irritable bowel syndrome, and these changes are associated with clinically meaningful improvement in symptoms and quality of life.
"A low FODMAP diet appears to be more effective than standard dietary advice for symptom control in IBS."
"Frequency of celiac disease and irritable bowel syndrome coexistance and its influence on the disease course"
A combination of curcumin and fennel essential oil significantly improved symptoms and quality of life in IBS patients over 30 days.
A combination of Melissa (Lemonbalm), Mentha spicata, Coriandrum sativum, Psyllium appears to have therapeutic value in treating IBS.
A gluten-free diet may influence symptoms in a subset of patients with IBS.
A mixture of Escherichia coli (DSM 17252) and Enterococcus faecalis (DSM 16440) has a therapeutic effect in the treatment of the irritable bowel syndrome.
A multispecies probiotic seems to be an effective and safe option to alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome, and to stabilize the intestinal microbiota.
A novel PO formulation designed for sustained release in the small intestine is a safe, effective treatment capable of providing rapid relief of IBS symptoms.
A pooled analysis of two small studies suggests a possible benefit for clinical homeopathy over placebo for people with constipation-predominant IBS.
A probiotic mixture is effective in alleviating irritable bowel syndrome symptoms.
A randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled clinical trial on Lactobacillus-containing cultured milk drink as adjuvant therapy for depression in irritable bowel syndrome.
A subgroup of Irritable Bowel Syndrome patients have latent/potential celiac disease and respond to a gluten-free diet.
A symbiotic formulation containing Lactobacillus paracasei has a therapeutic effect in patients with diarrhea predominant IBS.
Abdominal acupuncture is more effective for irritable bowel syndrome diarrhea than western medication.
Acupuncture combined with microorganism pharmaceutical preparations has a better therapeutic effect on irritable bowel syndrome of constipation type.
Acupuncture/moxibustion treatment shows promise in the area of symptom management for IBS.
Aloe vera may have therapeutic value in the treatment of irritable bowel syndrome.
Arrowroot is an effective treatment for diarrhea in irritable bowel syndrome patients.
Artichoke reduces symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome - Article 1.
Artichoke reduces symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome - Article 2.
Auricular acupressure was effective for symptom improvement in patients with irritable bowel syndrome.
Auricular acupressure was effective for symptom improvement in patients with irritable bowel syndrome.

B infantis 35624 alleviates symptoms in IBS; this symptomatic response was associated with normalization of the ratio of an anti-inflammatory to a proinflammatory cytokine, suggesting an immune-modulating role for this organism, in this disorder.
B. infantis is a probiotic that specifically relieves many of the symptoms of IBS.
Bacillus coagulans significantly improves abdominal pain and bloating in patients with IBS
Bacillus subtilis and Streptococcus faecium are a safe and useful probiotic agent for the treatment of abdominal pain in patients with IBS.
Beneficial effect of Lactobacillus gasseri BNR17 on irritable bowel syndrome.
Beneficial effects on abdominal bloating with an innovative food-grade formulation of Curcuma longa and Boswellia serrata extracts in subjects with irritable bowel syndrome.
Bifidobacterium animalis has a beneficial effect on health-related quality of life and symptoms in adults with IBS.
Biofeedback may have therapeutic value in the treatment of IBS.
Casperome supplementation could represent a promising alternative approach to manage symptoms associated with IBS.
Charcoal tablets have therapeutic value in the treatment of patients with irritable bowel syndrome.
Consumption of 2 green kiwifruits daily improves constipation and abdominal comfort.

remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: IBS is chronic and remitting; spontaneous 'remission' (symptom resolution without treatment) in ~30-40% at 2 years but symptom recurrence common; 10-20% develop persistent daily symptoms; <10% achieve permanent remission
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: Low-FODMAP diet: 50-80% symptomatic response; gut-directed hypnotherapy: 70-80% response, durable at 5 years; CBT: 60-70% response; rifaximin for IBS-D: 40% vs 31% placebo (modest but significant); neuromodulators (amitriptyline, nortriptyline): 60-70% response in IBS-D/IBS-M
remission.gap_size: Large — effective interventions exist but access to diet counselling, hypnotherapy, and CBT is limited; overreliance on antispasmodics and antidiarrheals
remission.barrier_detail: Gut-directed hypnotherapy has the strongest long-term evidence of any IBS treatment but virtually no trained therapists; FODMAP diet requires skilled dietitian; pharmacotherapy (linaclotide, plecanatide, eluxadoline) effective but expensive; most patients manage with OTC antispasmodics indefinitely
remission.last_soc_change: 2022 — AGA guidelines updated with neuromodulators as first-line; 2014 linaclotide/lubiprostone approved; 2015 eluxadoline for IBS-D; 2015 rifaximin for IBS-D; antispasmodics (hyoscine, mebeverine) essentially unchanged since 1960s-1970s
remission.outdated_treatments: Long-term loperamide as sole management in IBS-D without psychological or dietary intervention; alosetron (Lotronex — restricted prescribing programme due to ischaemic colitis; 5-HT3 antagonist with complex risk management); opioids for IBS pain (narcotic bowel syndrome risk — worsens visceral hypersensitivity with chronic use)
remission.adjunctive_emerging: Low-FODMAP diet as primary (not just adjunct); fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) — RCTs conflicting but some positive for IBS-D; gut-directed hypnotherapy (Manchester protocol — most durable intervention in literature); bile acid sequestrants (colesevelam) for bile acid malabsorption subtype; peppermint oil (enteric-coated — meta-analysis superior to placebo, natural antispasmodic)
remission.frontier_signal: FMT for IBS (PROFIT trial Phase 3); microbiome-targeted probiotics (VSL#3, Lactobacillus reuteri — inconsistent but growing evidence); tryptophan/serotonin pathway modulation; mast cell stabilisers (ebastine) for histamine-mediated IBS subtype; colonic sensory modulation via TRPV1 antagonists
